summary: DeVault argues that the term 'open source' has a specific, well-established meaning defined by the OSI's Open Source Definition, and that attempts to redefine it are fundamentally dishonest. He compares rebranding non-OSD-compliant software as 'open source' to trademark dilution, arguing it's motivated by financial self-interest. He acknowledges that language evolves but contends the current definition is both useful and morally just, since open source projects are collectively owned by their contributors. He concludes that alternative models like 'source available' are legitimate but must earn their own brand identity rather than co-opting the open source name.
key_points: Most arguments for redefining 'open source' are motivated by financial self-interest, making good faith difficult to presume
The OSD definition is useful precisely because it provides a quick shorthand for understanding the rights and values associated with software
Once a project receives outside contributions, it is collectively owned and the right to commercial exploitation should be shared with the community
A trademark is not required for language misuse to be dishonest — the OSD reflects the mainstream, established usage of the term
Alternative models like 'source available' are valid but must build their own brand rather than co-opting 'open source'
money_quotes: Most people arguing in favor of an unorthodox definition of 'open source' have a vested interest in their alternative view.

To disregard this in the interests of a financial incentive is dishonest, much like I would be dishonest for selling 'cakes' and fulfilling orders with used car tires with 'cake' written on them instead.
Language is defined by its usage, and the OSD is the popular usage of the term 'open source', without relying on the trademark system.
I am not prepared to accept a new definition which removes or reduces important rights in service of your private financial interests.
They are not rightfully yours once you receive outside contributions.
If you wish to access all of these benefits of the open source model, you must play by the open source rules.
Open source is not just eating the world, but has eaten the world.
Your movement has as much right to success as the open source movement, but you need to earn that success independently of the open source movement.
key_insight: Redefining 'open source' to exclude user rights is not linguistic evolution but dishonest brand appropriation driven by commercial self-interest.
